coredata.dc:title: Pottasium triiodide enhanced turn-off sensing of tyrosine in carbon dot platform
coredata.dc:creator: Nebu, John
Anu, K. Soman
Anjali Devi, J.S.
Aparna, R.S.
Aswathy, A.O.
Lekha, G.M.
Sony, George
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a turn-off sensing approach for simple and sensitive determination of tyrosine is developed on the basis of quenching of fluorescence of carbon dot. When carbon dot is mixed with potassium triiodide, the fluorescence of carbon dot undergo quenching and this quenching phenomena get enhanced in the presence of tyrosine. The possible interfering amino acids and biomolecules exhibit turn-on behaviour and thus tyrosine has good selectivity. The limit of detection of tyrosine is 950 nM. Real sample analysis in serum sample shows good recovery percentage.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Amino acid
Quenching
Paper strip assay
Real sample analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Quenching of NCDs with KI3 solution
Quenching of NCDs with KI3-Tyrosine system
Time correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) studies

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–Visible spectrum of NCDs. (B) (a) Excitation and (b) emission spectra of NCDs. (C) FTIR spectra of citric acid, urea and NCDs. (D) TEM image showing the diameter of NCDs. (E) DLS measurement showing the hydrodynamic diameter of NCDs.

(A) UV –Visible spectrum of NCDs- KI3 quenched system. (B) Photoluminescence spectra of NCDs by the addition of KI3 (concentration of I2 ranges from 0 to 14 μM.) (C) Linear plot of KI3 quenched NCDs. (D) DLS measurement showing the hydrodynamic diameter of NCDs-KI3 system.

(A) UV–Visible absorption spectrum of NCDs- KI3-Tyr system. Fluorescence spectra of NCDs quenched with (1: 6 = KI: I2) KI3-Tyr (B) 1.32 μM I2, (C) 1.97 μM I2 and (D) 14 μM I2. Inset (B) fluorescence turn-on of NCDs-KI3 system on the addition of 0.93 μM and 1.02 μM Tyr. Inset (C) fluorescence turn-on of NCDs-KI3 system by the addition of 0.93 μM Tyr. (E) Linear plot for fluorescence response of KI3-Tyr quenched NCDs system. (F) TEM images of NCDs -KI3-Tyr quenched system.
(A) Selectivity of Tyr among other interferent molecules. (B) Selectivity of Tyr among amino acids and biomolecules.
The interference study of various amino acids and biomolecules with tyrosine.
TCSPC analysis of NCDs, NCDs-KI3 system and NCDs-KI3-Tyr system. The concentration of I2 = 14 μM and Tyr = 2.614 μM are used for TCSPC analysis.
The variation of fluorescence intensity with change in pH of Tyrosine quenched NCDs-KI3 system.

(A) Excitation (green line) and emission (olive line) spectra of NCDs, absorption spectra of KI3 (magenta line), Tyr (red) and KI3-Tyr (blue). (B) UV–Visible absorption spectra of NCDs, NCDs-KI3 and NCD-KI3-Tyr quenched system. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
The paper strip shows fluorescence of NCDs (left), fluorescence turn-off behaviour of NCDs in the presence of KI3 (middle), KI3-Tyr system (right) in (A) visible light and (B) UV-radiation.
The tyrosine content of spiked human serum sample.

head.ce:title: Pottasium triiodide enhanced turn-off sensing of tyrosine in carbon dot platform

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, School of Physical and Mathematical Sciences, University of Kerala, Kariavattom, Trivandrum 695581, Kerala, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physical and Mathematical Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a turn-off sensing approach for simple and sensitive determination of tyrosine is developed on the basis of quenching of fluorescence of carbon dot. When carbon dot is mixed with potassium triiodide, the fluorescence of carbon dot undergo quenching and this quenching phenomena get enhanced in the presence of tyrosine. The possible interfering amino acids and biomolecules exhibit turn-on behaviour and thus tyrosine has good selectivity. The limit of detection of tyrosine is 950 nM. Real sample analysis in serum sample shows good recovery percentage.

The graphical abstract depicting the fluorescence turn off sensing of tyrosine using KI3 quenched nitrogen doped carbon dot.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: Tyrosine (Tyr) plays a major role in clinical chemistry and can be directly correlated to several metabolic and mental disorders. Tyr is an essential aromatic amino acid, which plays a vital role in maintaining a positive nitrogen balance in our body [1]. Tyr is synthesized in the body via the catabolism of phenylalanine [2]. Tyr acts as precursors for catecholamines and hormones such as dopamine, norepinephrine, epinephrine, melamine and thyroid hormones [1,3]. The skin cells convert Tyr into melamine, which protects living beings from the harmful ultraviolet radiation. Tyr acts as an oral cancer biomarker. Deficiency of Tyr may result in depression, albinism, alkaptonuria and other psychological diseases [4]. Moreover, high levels of Tyr is related to Tyrosinemia disease, which occurs due to the disorder of Tyr metabolism [5]. Symptoms include kidney and liver disturbance and intellectual disability. In Type I and Type II tyrosinemia, Type I cause damage to kidney and liver. Tyrosine accumulates cause ophthalmologic and dermatologic abnormalities. Type I and II detected via blood test, Type II detected from the presence of significantly elevated plasma tyrosine levels [6]. Type III is rare in case [7]. The increased level of Tyr oxidation product is associated with diseases such as atherosclerosis, Alzheimer's disease and Parkinson's diseases [8]. Here arises a need to develop a simple sensing method for the determination of Tyr in real samples.

Numerous methods are available for the detection of Tyr. The usual methods include chromatography, high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [9], electrochemical methods [10], spectrophotometry and upconversion lumeniscence methods [11]. All these methods use tedious procedure, expensive instruments and are time consuming. Among these methods, fluorometry sensing method offers sensitive and simple procedures.

Carbon dots based fluorescent probes are emerging as a detection strategy for many cations including Fe2+ [12], Hg2+ [13], Zn2+ [14], Al3+ [15], Ag+ [16], K+ [17], Be2+ [18] as well as number of anions including oxalate [19], iodide [20], hypochlorous acid [21] and nitrite [22]. Carbon dots quenching facilitated by electron transfer has been used for the detection of Hg2+ by Zhu et al [23]. Carbon dots have been used for sensing small molecules and drugs, including tetracycline hydrogen peroxide, melamine [24], picric acid [25] and amoxicillin [25]. Carbon dots containing systems have been used to detect the wide range of biomolecules such as glutathione, hydrogen sulfide, L-cysteine, ascorbic acid, cholesterol, dopamine and glucose. This is also done by fluorescence quenching, fluorescence turn on methods, modified electrodes and target recognition [26].

In this work, the selective sensing of Tyr is obtained by utilizing the accelerated fluorescence turn-off behavior of KI3 incorporated carbon dots by adding Tyr. For this, nitrogen doped carbon dot was synthesized, which show high fluorescence. In order to make it a good probe, quenching efficiency of KI3 solution on carbon dot was studied. It was found that carbon dot was efficiently quenched by KI3 solution. The partially quenched KI3 incorporated carbon dots system was further allowed to interact with L-tyrosine. The studies reveal that KI3 incorporated partially quenched carbon dot system undergoes enhanced quenching with L-tyrosine. Hence, this system can be used for the detection of tyrosine. We had conducted the interference studies of various co-existing amino acids and biomolecules. Moreover, we attempt the same sensing procedure in blood samples. Good results were obtained with high dilution.

Citric acid anhydrous pure, potassium chloride and glycine purified were brought from Merck Specialities Pvt. Ltd., Mumbai (www.merk.co.in). Sodium chloride, urea, l-ascorbic acid and potassium iodide were bought from NICE Chemical Pvt. Ltd., Kochi (www.nicechemicals.com). d-glucose anhydrous and iodine were purchased from SRL Pvt. Ltd., Mumbai (www.srlchem.com). Sodium hydroxide, creatine, aspartic acid and glutamic acid were brought from Spectrum Chemicals, India (www.spectrochemical.com). Glutathione (≥98%) and histidine were purchased from Sigma Aldrich (www.sigmaaldrich.com). L-tryptophan and L-tyrosine were purchased from CDH, India. L-proline 99%, Serine, and Conc.HCl were obtained from Alfa Aesar (www.alfa.com). l-lysine was purchased from CHEMIE Pvt. Ltd., Mumbai, India. L-cysteine, haemoglobin, L- Leucine, homocysteine, methionine and valine were purchased from TCI Tokyo, Japan (www.tcichemicals.com).

The room temperature fluorescence measurements of all samples were studied using Jasco FP-750 Spectroflourometer. High Resolution TEM images of NCDs were taken in a JEOL JEM 2100 multipurpose apparatus, at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV analytical electron microscope. DLS measurements were carried out in HORIBA Scientific nanoparticles analyser SZ-100. The UV–Visible spectroscopic study was done using Perkin Elmer Lamda 365 UV–Visible spectrophotometer. The functional group analysis were carried out in ATR-FTIR by using Agilent technologies Cary 630 FTIR spectrometer. Digital pH meter MK VI of Systronics was used to measure pH. The samples were prepared by microwave irradiation using POWER SOLO 17D, ONIDA instrument. Fluorescence lifetimes of all samples were carried out in Fluorolog Fluorescence spectrophotometer, Horiba, USA using time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC).

NCDs were synthesized via microwave irradiation method. Citric acid (1.5 g) was mixed with urea (1.5 g) in 5 mL water. It was then irradiated with microwave radiation at 280 W for 2 min in a domestic microwave oven. A syrupy solid obtained was further diluted in 10 mL water [27]. Intensity was measured using Spectrofluorometer JASCO–FP 750. UV–Visible spectral studies, photoluminescence, TEM, Time correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) and FTIR studies of NCDs were also conducted.

Previously optimized KI3 solution was prepared. Iodine (0.32 g) and KI (1.35 g) dissolved in 25 mL water. Fluorescence of NCDs were modified with the KI3 solution (concentration of I2 ranges from 0.66 μM–3.25 μM). Quenched fluorescence intensity and corresponding wavelength were recorded. On step wise addition of KI3, change in fluorescence intensity was noted.

A 25 mL aqueous solution of L-tyrosine (100 μM) was prepared. The as prepared tyrosine solution was added to the modified NCDs-KI3 system. About 3 mL of NCDs dispersion is mixed with (optimized KI3, KI: I2 = 1: 6 ratio) KI3 (concentration of I2 as 1.32 μM, 1.97 μM and 14 μM) and Tyr is added to it (0.931 μM–5.3542 μM). Fluorescence intensity of the NCDs-KI3-Tyr was recorded after each addition. Here, 14 μM KI3 and 2.614 μM tyrosine were used for further selectivity and sensitivity studies.

Selectivity studies of Tyr among amino acids and biomolecules were conducted by using 14 μM KI3 and 2.614 μM of different biomolecules such as ascorbic acid, aspartic acid, lysine, glycine, L-tryptophan, L-proline, valine, arginine, glutathione, L-cysteine, homocysteine, serine, histidine, glutamic acid, L- leucine, methionine, and other interference molecules like d-glucose, creatine and urea. Sensitivity studies were conducted with the same molecules. Here 14 μM KI3 and 2.614 μM Tyr were used for further selectivity and interference studies, which is comparable to the concentration of amino acids in the human blood serum. Interference of various amino acids and biomolecules in the quenching nature of Tyr was studied with NCDs by adding 14 μM KI3 and 2.614 μM amino acids in the presence of 2.614 μM Tyr.
Tyrosine quenching efficiency were measured at different pH levels such as acidic, neutral, basic maintained by using 0.1 M HCl, 0.1 M NaOH syrup and PBS buffer (pH 7.32).

Blood sample was collected from a healthy person's body. From 100 μM stock solution, Tyr was spiked in diluted serum. It was then mixed with NCDs solution to get an effective concentration of 1.61 μM and 3.17 μM. Fluorescence intensity of the Tyr added samples with partially quenched NCDs- KI3 system were recorded. Limit of detection (LOD) and percentage of recovery were measured.

Whatman No-40 filter papers were used for the detection of Tyr quenching. It was soaked with NCDs solution and dried. Another paper strip was soaked with NCDs-KI3 and third with NCDs-KI3-Tyr mixtures and dried. These paper strips were irradiated with UV radiation in a UV chamber. From the fluorescence observation, it is clear that the method ensures practical feasibility of the sensor.

UV–Visible absorption spectrum of nitrogen doped carbon dots (NCDs) in aqueous dispersion is depicted in Fig. 1(A). The spectrum peak obtained at 338 nm indicates n-π* transition due to the presence of polar surface groups such as CN, CO and C-OH groups in NCDs [28]. The existance of a small shoulder peak around 260 nm occurs due to the presence of π-π* transition of inner core of the NCDs. The photoluminescence is one of the fascinating features of NCDs. Here excitation wavelength (λex) of NCDs depends on the emission wavelength and intensity. This is because of optical selection of differently sized nanoparticle, or presence of different emissive traps on the surface of NCDs [29]. The excitation and emission fluorescence spectra of NCDs show maximum excitation at 387 nm and emission at 488 nm (Fig. 1 B), which indicates the fluorescence property of prepared NCDs.

The FTIR spectrum was used to identify the functional groups on the surface of NCDs. FTIR spectra of citric acid, urea and NCDs were obtained (Fig. 2C). Peaks obtained for citric acid at 3490 cm−1, 3278 cm−1, 2648 cm−1 and 2662 cm−1 indicate OH stretch. Broad peaks obtained for urea at 3423 cm−1 is due to N- H stretch, peaks at 3323 cm−1 and 3252 cm−1 indicates primary and secondary amine groups. Broad peak at 1676 cm−1 indicate NH bending. NCDs shows peaks at 3420 cm−1, 3326 cm−1 and 3186 cm−1. From the FTIR data, it is clear that the prepared NCDs exist in aqueous medium and peaks are different from citric acid and urea.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were used to characterize the size and morphology of NCDs. As shown in the Fig. 1D, NCDs have a diameter around 3.355 ± 0.764 nm and exhibit spherical shape [30]. DLS measurement showing the average hydrodynamic diameter of NCDs (Fig. 1E) is 70.2 nm. Zeta potential value is −1.7 mV (Fig. S1A) [31].

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were used to characterize the size and morphology of NCDs. As shown in the Fig. 1D, NCDs have a diameter around 3.355 ± 0.764 nm and exhibit spherical shape [30]. DLS measurement showing the average hydrodynamic diameter of NCDs (Fig. 1E) is 70.2 nm. Zeta potential value is −1.7 mV (Fig. S1A) [31].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quenching of NCDs with KI3 solution
ce:section.ce:para: Prepared KI3 solution was added to the carbon dot. UV – Visible spectrum of NCDs- KI3 quenched system obtained is shown as Fig. 2A. In the spectrum, peak at 349 nm shows n-π* transition within the NCDs, which is retained in the NCDs-KI3 system. Fluorescence intensity of carbon dot decreases with the addition of KI3 solution (Fig. 2B). The quenching efficiency of KI3 solution mainly reported due to the heavy atom effect of iodine caused by high molecular weight [32]. It is found that 14 μM KI3 solution results in 63.6% quenching. An excellent linear response relationship y = 0.11[KI3] + 1.07, (R2 = 0.9884) was observed (Fig. 2C). The limit of detection (LOD) of KI3 was determined by the equation 3σ/slope, where σ is the standard deviation of response. LOD of the KI3 quenching obtained is 685 nM.

From the DLS data (Fig. 2D), hydrodynamic diameter of the quenched system obtained is 130.7 nm, which is due to the hydrophilic nature of NCDs-KI3 system. Zeta potential measurements reveals the negative value of −30.9 mV, due to the phenomenon of pH induced deprotonation of the surface functional groups. NCDs has a neutral surface charge. After treated with KI3, zeta potential reduced to −30.9 mV [31]. It may due to the adsorption of I3 − ion on the surface of NCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quenching of NCDs with KI3-Tyrosine system
ce:section.ce:para: UV –Visible absorption spectrum of quenched NCDs-KI3- Tyr shows peaks at 350 nm and 274 nm, which in turn indicate that n-π* transition and π-π* transition of surface groups (Fig. 3A). Here the peak due to π-π* transition get enhanced as compared to NCDs and NCDs-KI3 system. The red shift of the peak at 274 nm arised due to the influence of Tyr. The quenching of NCDs with KI3-Tyr system analysed with three different concentration of KI3. In the first case, 1.32 μM (concentration of I2) KI3 used and further Tyr was (0.93 μM to 5.35 μM) added (Fig. 3B). The first two addition of Tyr gave an increase in intensity of NCDs-KI3 system (Inset: Fig. 3B). Here Tyr attack KI3 adsorbed on the surface of NCDs and hence its quenching efficiency decreased, which results in increase of fluorescence intensity of NCDs-KI3 system. On further addition of tyrosine, a complex is formed between tyr and KI3, which results in the enhanced quenching of NCDs. Again when the concentration of KI3 increased (Concentration of I2 = 1.97 μM), only the first addition of Tyr (0.93 μM) results in the increase of fluorescence intensity of NCDs-KI3 system (Inset: Fig. 3C). This is because, excess of KI3, which is not adsorbed on NCDs made strong complex with Tyr and hence the KI3 present on the surface of NCDs is not much attacked by Tyr. Compared to the first and second, in the third case, turn on intensity was not observed, which is due to the presence of excess KI3 that remains unadsorbed on the surface of NCDs (Fig. 3D). This is a clear evidence of the formation of complex between KI3 and Tyr, which get adsorbed on the surface of NCDs and results in almost 100% quenching of fluorescence of NCDs. Here 14 μM KI3 and 2.614 μM Tyr were used for further selectivity and sensitivity studies, which is comparable to the concentration of amino acids in the human blood serum. Linear plot of quenching of NCDs-KI3 probe by adding Tyr from 0.931 μM–5.35 μM was obtained (y = 21.802 [Tyr] + 1.158), (R2 = 0.994) (Fig. 3E) with a limit of detection (LOD) of 950 nM. LOD of Tyr is calculated using the equation 3σ/k (σ = 6.904 μM and k = 21.8), where σ = standard deviation and k = slope (Fig.3E).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of NCDs-KI3-Tyr quenched system shows an increase in the particle size compared to the TEM images of NCDs. The diameter of the particle is around 8.58 ± 1.55 nm (Fig.3F). DLS and Zeta potential value of NCDs- KI3-Tyr quenched sytem were 101.8 nm (Fig. S1D) and −0.4 mV (Fig. S1C) respectively. Here the hydrodynamic diameter of NCDs- KI3-Tyr is less than that of NCDs- KI3, inscribed the less hydrophilic nature of it. The Zeta potential value is relatively close to zero, which results in the aggregation of NCDs.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of NCDs-KI3-Tyr quenched system shows an increase in the particle size compared to the TEM images of NCDs. The diameter of the particle is around 8.58 ± 1.55 nm (Fig.3F). DLS and Zeta potential value of NCDs- KI3-Tyr quenched sytem were 101.8 nm (Fig. S1D) and −0.4 mV (Fig. S1C) respectively. Here the hydrodynamic diameter of NCDs- KI3-Tyr is less than that of NCDs- KI3, inscribed the less hydrophilic nature of it. The Zeta potential value is relatively close to zero, which results in the aggregation of NCDs.

Quenching behaviour of Tyr is clearly determined from the selectivity studies. 14 μM KI3 (concentration of I2) and 2.614 μM of aminoacids and biomolecules are added individually. Among these, Tyr clearly shows selective turn off behaviour, while other molecules shows fluorescence enhancement (Fig. 4(A), (B)).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Time correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) studies
ce:section.ce:para: TCSPC studies are used to get the life time of NCDs. The life time of NCDs obtained as 9.78 ns (Fig. 6 ). The life time of NCDs indicates the measure of the time that electrons in NCDs spend in the excited state before fluorescence emission. Fluorescence decay interpreted in terms of exponential factors and decay time. Average life time of the NCDs-KI3 system was 8.55 ns. Although there is an insignificant decrease in life time for NCDs-KI3 quenched system compared to that of NCDs, there is no appreciable change. From the observations, it is clear that quenching is static in nature. Moreover, the average life time of NCD-KI3-Tyr is 7.22 ns. On comparing TCSPC analysis of NCDs, NCD-KI3 and NCD-KI3-Tyr system it is clear that quenching is static in nature [34].

In NCDs-KI3-Tyr system, the intensity is independent of pH, although there is slight increase in fluorescence intensity in the basic condition (Fig. 7 ). This slight variation is due to the interaction of phenolic group of Tyr with NaOH in the basic condition.

NCDs are prepared from anydrous citric acid and urea, by the microwave synthesis. When NCDs react with KI3, quenching occurs. This is mainly due to the heavy atom effect of iodine. It is well known that iodine mainly existed as IO3 −, I2 and I−. Among these, NCDs have selective response to I2. Therefore IO3 − and I− translation to I2 should be included before the reaction [8]. As shown in Fig. 8A, the absence of overlap of the absorbance spectra of KI3 and Tyr with the emission spectra of NCDs rules out the chance of Froster resonance energy transfer (FRET). In addition to this, since there is a small overlap between the absorbance peak of KI3 and Tyr with the excitation spectrum of NCDs, implies a chance for secondary inner filter effect. In UV–Visible spectrum of NCDs-KI3-Tyr system, a peak of NCDs at 274 nm similar to the peak of Tyr get modified (Fig. 8B). The quenching analysis (in three different manner) of Tyr reveals complex formation between KI3 and Tyr (Fig. 3B, C,D). From the TCSPC results, it is clear that there is no appreciable change in life time of NCDs and the quenched system, which implies the static quenching of NCDs. Hence there is a chance of complex formation on the surface of NCDs. In the TEM image of NCDs- KI3-Tyr quenched system, the increase in size indicate complex formation between KI3 and Tyr on the surface of NCDs (Fig. 3F).

Real sample analysis was conducted using Tyr spiked in human serum. 1.61 and 3.17 μM Tyr spiked with 100 times diluted serum. From the analysis, (Table 1 ) better percentage of recovery are obtained for 1.61 μM Tyr in 100 times diluted serum sample.

The paper strip analysis shows turn-off behaviour of NCDs by adding KI3 and Tyr in the visible light and UV radiation as shown in Fig. 9 . Here the results shows the potentialities of the method for the selective sensing of Tyr in a simple manner.

In the present study, NCDs are prepared from citric acid and urea. Prepared KI3 is used to quench NCDs. This quenching phenomena is further enhanced by the addition of tyrosine molecules. Limit of detection of Tyr is determined. Tyr shows better selectivity and sensitivity response among other amino acids and biomolecules. The quenching mechanism is explained based on TCSPC analysis, TEM images and photoluminescence spectra. This NCDs-KI3 system is used for sensing of tyrosine in serum samples and better recovery percentage obtained. Paper strip assay ensures the practical feasibility of sensor.

The following are the supplementary data related to this article.
Fig. S1
Zeta potential of (A) NCDs, (B) NCDs-KI3 system and (C) NCDs- KI3-Tyr quenched system. (D) DLS measurement of NCDs-KI3-Tyr quenched system.
Fig. S1
Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2018.12.021.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The first and second authors had made equal contribution. The authors thank the Head, Department of Chemistry, University of Kerala, Kariavattom Campus, Thiruvananthapuram for pursuing the platform to conduct the research. The authors also thank the Director, SICC, University of Kerala, Kariavattom campus, Thiruvananthapuram; Director, SAIF-STIC-CUSAT, Kochi; DST-SAIF, M.G. University, Kottayam.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrocatalytic oxidation of tyrosine at poly(threonine)-film modified carbon paste electrode and its voltammetric determination in real samples
Understanding and applying tyrosine biochemical diversity
Tyrosine, phenylalanine, and catecholamine synthesis and function in the brain
Electrochemical determination of tyrosine in the presence of dopamine and uric acid at the surface of gold nanoparticles modified carbon paste electrode
Filtered carbon nanotubes-based electrodes for rapid sensing and monitoring of L-tyrosine in plasma and whole blood samples
Diagnosis and treatment of tyrosinemia type I: a US and Canadian consensus group review and recommendations
Tyrosinemia type III in an asymptomatic girl
High-performance liquid chromatography with fluorescence detection for quantitation of tryptophan and tyrosine in a shrimp waste protein concentrate
J. Chromatogr. B Analyt. Technol. Biomed. Life Sci.
A facile sensitive l-tyrosine electrochemical sensor based on a coupled CuO/Cu2O nanoparticles and multi-walled carbon nanotubes nanocomposite film
Enzymatic-induced upconversion photoinduced electron transfer for sensing tyrosine in human serum
Plant leaf-derived fluorescent carbon dots for sensing, patterning and coding
Hydrothermal synthesis of highly fluorescent carbon nanoparticles from sodium citrate and their use for the detection of mercury ions
Quinoline derivative-functionalized carbon dots as a fluorescent nanosensor for sensing and intracellular imaging of Zn2+
High photoluminescent carbon nanodots and quercetin-Al3+ construct a ratiometric fluorescent sensing system
Growth and stabilization of silver nanoparticles on carbon dots and sensing application
Sensing metal ions with ion selectivity of a crown ether and fluorescence resonance energy transfer between carbon dots and graphene
Engineering surface states of carbon dots to achieve controllable luminescence for solid-luminescent composites and sensitive Be(2+) detection
A fluorescent turn-off/on method for detection of Cu2+and oxalate using carbon dots as fluorescent probes in aqueous solution
Carbon dots-based fluorescent probes for sensitive and selective detection of iodide
N-doped carbon dots with high sensitivity and selectivity for hypochlorous acid detection and its application in water
Nitrite sensing based on the carbon dots-enhanced chemiluminescence from peroxynitrous acid and carbonate
Carbon dots production via pyrolysis of sago waste as potential probe for metal ions sensing
A carbon dot based biosensor for melamine detection by fluorescence resonance energy transfer
Highly sensitive and selective detection of amoxicillin using carbon quantum dots derived from beet
A dual-mode nanosensor based on carbon quantum dots and gold nanoparticles for discriminative detection of glutathione in human plasma
A biocompatible fluorescent Ink based on water-soluble luminescent carbon nanodots
Carbon quantum dots: synthesis, properties and applications
Highly Photoluminescent carbon dots for multicolor patterning, sensors, and bioimaging
A biocompatible fluorescent ink based on water-soluble luminescent carbon nanodots
pH-elicited luminescence functionalities of carbon dots: mechanistic insights

Facile bulk production of highly blue fluorescent graphitic carbon nitride quantum dots and their application as highly selective and sensitive sensors for the detection of mercuric and iodide ions in aqueous media
Determination of iodide via direct fluorescence quenching at nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot fluorophores
